category_summary:
The day's biggest story was **Yann LeCun** [unveiling **AMI Labs**](/?date=2026-03-11&category=social#item-d2042c0dde07), a new AI startup with a record-breaking **$1.03B seed round** and [**$4.5B+ valuation**](/?date=2026-03-11&category=social#item-6502ee84d04e), pursuing non-LLM approaches to AI. This crystallized a broader trend: **Ethan Mollick** noted over half a dozen well-funded companies from famous researchers are now betting against LLM-only paradigms.

- **Demis Hassabis** [marked **AlphaGo's** 10th anniversary](/?date=2026-03-11&category=social#item-cbb109aa4f1d) with rare strategic candor, [outlining **DeepMind's** AGI roadmap](/?date=2026-03-11&category=social#item-739af9758236) as first "AlphaGo-like" then "AlphaZero-like," with the latter likely coming post-AGI
- **Google** made two major product announcements: [**Gemini Embedding 2**](/?date=2026-03-11&category=social#item-60208d8869a1) (SOTA multimodal embeddings across text, images, video, and audio) and [**Gemini-powered Workspace**](/?date=2026-03-11&category=social#item-86540fc413c9) integration across Docs, Sheets, Slides, and Drive
- **Anthropic's** Claude Code team [shipped the **/btw** command](/?date=2026-03-11&category=social#item-904e52c7c093) for side-chain conversations, going massively viral with over 1M views
- **Soumith Chintala's Thinking Machines Lab** announced a [**1GW+ NVIDIA Vera Rubin**](/?date=2026-03-11&category=social#item-190eccef50f0) compute partnership, signaling unprecedented infrastructure scale for startups
- **François Chollet** [predicted AI agents](/?date=2026-03-11&category=social#item-23b1df6aec70) will soon become autonomous economic actors, while **Mollick** [argued current AI capabilities](/?date=2026-03-11&category=social#item-f303df4cc482) alone could transform white-collar work for a decade even if development stopped today
category_summary_html:
The day's biggest story was Yann LeCun unveiling AMI Labs, a new AI startup with a record-breaking $1.03B seed round and $4.5B+ valuation, pursuing non-LLM approaches to AI. This crystallized a broader trend: Ethan Mollick noted over half a dozen well-funded companies from famous researchers are now betting against LLM-only paradigms.

  • Demis Hassabis marked AlphaGo's 10th anniversary with rare strategic candor, outlining DeepMind's AGI roadmap as first "AlphaGo-like" then "AlphaZero-like," with the latter likely coming post-AGI
  • Google made two major product announcements: Gemini Embedding 2 (SOTA multimodal embeddings across text, images, video, and audio) and Gemini-powered Workspace integration across Docs, Sheets, Slides, and Drive
  • Anthropic's Claude Code team shipped the /btw command for side-chain conversations, going massively viral with over 1M views
  • Soumith Chintala's Thinking Machines Lab announced a 1GW+ NVIDIA Vera Rubin compute partnership, signaling unprecedented infrastructure scale for startups
  • François Chollet predicted AI agents will soon become autonomous economic actors, while Mollick argued current AI capabilities alone could transform white-collar work for a decade even if development stopped today

themes.name: AMI Labs Launch
themes.description: Yann LeCun officially announces AMI Labs, a new AI startup with a record $1.03B seed round and $4.5B+ post-money valuation, pursuing non-LLM AI approaches. Backed by Hiro Capital (Nick Clegg), French institutions, and Emmanuel Macron's endorsement. Team includes Saining Xie and other notable researchers.

themes.name: Google Gemini Product Launches
themes.description: Major Google announcements including Gemini Embedding 2 (multimodal SOTA embeddings) and Gemini-powered Workspace integration across Docs, Sheets, Slides, and Drive. Both posts received massive engagement (~545K views each).

themes.name: AGI Strategy and Timelines
themes.description: Multiple leaders share AGI perspectives: Hassabis outlines AlphaGo→AlphaZero→post-AGI roadmap with safety emphasis; Emollick tracks weak AGI criteria being met; Emollick observes labs prioritize recursive improvement toward superintelligence above commercial products.

themes.name: Claude Code /btw Feature Launch
themes.description: Anthropic's Claude Code team (trq212) launched the /btw command enabling side-chain conversations during agent work. Massively viral (1.1M views). Technical details: single turn, no tool calls, full context access. Built by Erik Schluntz as side project.

themes.name: AI Startups & Major Funding
themes.description: Yann LeCun's AMI Labs exits stealth with $1B+ seed round for world models; Percy Liang announces Simile AI for society simulation; Thinking Machines Lab secures gigawatt-scale NVIDIA partnership

themes.name: Alternative AI Approaches
themes.description: Growing ecosystem of well-funded non-LLM startups (AMI Labs being the latest). Marcus notes AMI Labs is neurosymbolic. Emollick counts over half a dozen such companies, suggesting diversification of AI development pathways.

themes.name: NVIDIA-Thinky Machines Partnership
themes.description: Soumith Chintala (PyTorch co-creator) announced partnership with NVIDIA bringing 1GW+ compute via Vera Rubin architecture, with NVIDIA making significant investment in his startup Thinky Machines.

themes.name: Future of Work and AI Adoption
themes.description: Emollick argues current AI capabilities alone would transform white collar work over 5-10 years; urges choosing augmentation over pure automation. Chollet predicts AI agents becoming economic actors within 1-2 years.

themes.name: NVIDIA & Compute Infrastructure
themes.description: NVIDIA partners with Thinking Machines Lab for 1GW Vera Rubin deployment; Jensen Huang describes five-layer AI stack; massive NVIDIA post with 1.25M views suggests major announcement

themes.name: AI Agent Reliability & Failure Modes
themes.description: Real-world experiences with AI agents breaking in production, violating constraints, and the need for hybrid deterministic-LLM architectures to ensure reliability.

The core focus for the AI Labs really is "make the smartest model you can so it can make better models so it can make a superintelligence 1st." That is where the money goes

The fact that they ship a whole bunch of consumer and B2B products using those models is almost incidental

items.title:
I think it’s pretty clear that simulation is the next frontier for AI.

The most impressive feats of...
items.content:
I think it’s pretty clear that simulation is the next frontier for AI.

The most impressive feats of AI to date are when we have a clear environment + reward, whether it be beating Le Sedol at Go, winning an IMO gold medal, or writing entire apps from scratch. In these cases, the RL algorithm can try different actions, and observe the well-defined consequences in the safety of a docker container.

But what about messy real-world situations involving people? The rewards are unclear, the stakes are high, and you can’t experiment in the real world. But these situations are precisely where the next big opportunity in AI is. To crack this, we need to *simulate* society (“put society into a docker container”). Concretely, this means building a model that can predict what will happen in any given situation (real or hypothetical). If we can do this, we are only limited by our imagination: predict the future, optimize for better outcomes, answer hypothetical (“what if”) questions.  Ultimately, this goes beyond making better decisions, but it’s about giving us a better understanding of ourselves and the world.

Simulation is the whole enchilada.  And this is exactly the research that @simile_ai is working on.  Read more here:
https://t.co/eBMW2beHdT
items.author: @percyliang
items.summary: Percy Liang (Stanford) argues simulation is the next frontier for AI—building models that simulate society to predict outcomes and optimize decisions. Announces his startup Simile AI working on this.
items.reasoning: Highly respected Stanford AI professor making a substantive intellectual argument about the future of AI with a concrete startup announcement. Very high engagement (701 likes, 60K views). Original and thought-provoking thesis.
items.summary_html: Percy Liang (Stanford) argues simulation is the next frontier for AI—building models that simulate society to predict outcomes and optimize decisions. Announces his startup Simile AI working on this.
items.content_html:

The most impressive feats of AI to date are when we have a clear environment + reward, whether it be beating Le Sedol at Go, winning an IMO gold medal, or writing entire apps from scratch. Concretely, this means building a model that can predict what will happen in any given situation (real or hypothetical). items.title: Meta-benchmark idea: A collection of basic agent harnesses (AGENTS.md, docs, skills, hooks) that enc...
items.content:
Meta-benchmark idea: A collection of basic agent harnesses (AGENTS.md, docs, skills, hooks) that encapsulate non-trivial applications, each with a single prompt to terraform the application in one shot starting from scratch. When new coding agents or models come out, we can run against the harness to directly compare to previous model versions. “This model wrote a browser from scratch from the standard browser harness with X% feature completeness and only Y lines of code in Z hours for $XYZ.”

Imagine seeing that browser Cursor developed through the lens of each subsequent model update. How good was the code? How fast was the renderer? How complete was the feature set? How long did the agent have to run? What was the all-in cost of the tokens?

Kind of a CSS Zen Garden for agent harnesses, which did a ton to push forward browser standards.

I feel like all of us have experienced the difference in Codex 5.4 Extra High (if not, stop reading this post and try it immediately), but we still don’t have a way of capturing in a benchmark the qualitative experience of using it as a developer, particularly for measures like architecture and code quality.
items.author: @btaylor
items.summary: Bret Taylor proposes a 'meta-benchmark' for coding agents: standardized agent harnesses that test full application generation from scratch, comparing models on code quality, completeness, speed, and cost. Mentions Codex 5.4 Extra High as a qualitative leap.
items.reasoning: Practical and thoughtful proposal from Sierra CEO for benchmarking agentic coding. Validates Codex 5.4 Extra High's quality. 17K views. Addresses a real gap in evaluating coding agents.
items.summary_html: Bret Taylor proposes a 'meta-benchmark' for coding agents: standardized agent harnesses that test full application generation from scratch, comparing models on code quality, completeness, speed, and cost. Mentions Codex 5.4 Extra High as a qualitative leap.
items.content_html:
Meta-benchmark idea: A collection of basic agent harnesses (AGENTS.md, docs, skills, hooks) that encapsulate non-trivial applications, each with a single prompt to terraform the application in one shot starting from scratch. When new coding agents or models come out, we can run against the harness to directly compare to previous model versions. “This model wrote a browser from scratch from the standard browser harness with X% feature completeness and only Y lines of code in Z hours for $XYZ.”

Imagine seeing that browser Cursor developed through the lens of each subsequent model update. How good was the code? How fast was the renderer? How complete was the feature set? How long did the agent have to run? What was the all-in cost of the tokens?

Kind of a CSS Zen Garden for agent harnesses, which did a ton to push forward browser standards.

I feel like all of us have experienced the difference in Codex 5.4 Extra High (if not, stop reading this post and try it immediately), but we still don’t have a way of capturing in a benchmark the qualitative experience of using it as a developer, particularly for measures like architecture and code quality.

items.title:
A startup just locked in 1 gigawatt of compute. That's 10x a typical data center campus.

Thinking M...
items.content:
A startup just locked in 1 gigawatt of compute. That's 10x a typical data center campus.

Thinking Machines Lab, founded by ex-OpenAI CTO Mira Murati, just announced a multi-year deal with NVIDIA to deploy Vera Rubin systems at gigawatt scale.

> 1GW of next-gen compute secured
> Custom training + inference pipelines
> Open model access for enterprises and researchers
> NVIDIA made a direct investment in the startup

Here's what makes this different from a regular chip deal.

They're co-building training and serving systems tuned specifically to NVIDIA's architecture. Not just buying hardware, but optimizing the full stack from training runs to low-latency inference.

Until now, fine-tuning and customizing frontier models required resources only hyperscalers could afford. With Tinker, their fine-tuning API, now backed by gigawatt-scale infrastructure, that access starts moving toward enterprises and research labs.

https://t.co/5KD1lTlVF1

items.title:
I have an agent taking every new Stripe payment and inserting the information into a spreadsheet.

I...
items.content:
I have an agent taking every new Stripe payment and inserting the information into a spreadsheet.

It uses an MCP server to access the spreadsheet and determine the first empty row to write the new payment.

Everything was working fine until this morning.

When checking the contents of a spreadsheet column, the MCP server returns something like this:

[$1000, $1500, $1200, null, null, $100, $235, , , , , , ,]

Notice the null and the empty values (I'm not sure why there is a difference, but in the spreadsheet, both represent empty cells).

Today, my agent decided that `null` cells meant they weren't empty (they are).

Here is the funny part:

When the agent didn't find any empty cells within the hardcoded range I gave it to write new values, it expanded its search to the next set of rows.

I hardcoded: "Look for empty values within B2:B100". The agent didn't find empty values, so it asked the MCP to return any cells that weren't null between B100:B200.

Two huge problems:

1. It violated my requirements.
2. It made a mistake in the filter (non-null cells include cells with data!)

The agent overwrote existing data in the spreadsheet.

I realize this happened because I don't trust it. I didn't lose any data because Google Spreadsheets keeps a history of every change.

This is where we are right now.
items.author: @svpino
items.summary: Santiago details how his Stripe payment agent broke: it misinterpreted null vs empty cells in spreadsheet data from MCP, violated hardcoded range constraints by expanding search, used wrong filter logic, and overwrote existing data. Saved by Google Sheets version history.
items.reasoning: Excellent real-world case study of AI agent failure modes. Very high engagement (157 likes, 24.8K views). Illustrates critical issues: agents violating constraints, non-deterministic MCP data interpretation, cascading errors. Valuable cautionary tale for agent builders.
items.summary_html: Santiago details how his Stripe payment agent broke: it misinterpreted null vs empty cells in spreadsheet data from MCP, violated hardcoded range constraints by expanding search, used wrong filter logic, and overwrote existing data. Saved by Google Sheets version history.
items.content_html:
I have an agent taking every new Stripe payment and inserting the information into a spreadsheet.

It uses an MCP server to access the spreadsheet and determine the first empty row to write the new payment.

Everything was working fine until this morning.

When checking the contents of a spreadsheet column, the MCP server returns something like this:

[$1000, $1500, $1200, null, null, $100, $235, , , , , , ,]

Notice the null and the empty values (I'm not sure why there is a difference, but in the spreadsheet, both represent empty cells).

Today, my agent decided that `null` cells meant they weren't empty (they are).

Here is the funny part:

When the agent didn't find any empty cells within the hardcoded range I gave it to write new values, it expanded its search to the next set of rows.

I hardcoded: "Look for empty values within B2:B100". The agent didn't find empty values, so it asked the MCP to return any cells that weren't null between B100:B200.

Two huge problems:

1. It violated my requirements.

2. It made a mistake in the filter (non-null cells include cells with data!)

The agent overwrote existing data in the spreadsheet.

I realize this happened because I don't trust it. I didn't lose any data because Google Spreadsheets keeps a history of every change.

This is where we are right now.

items.title: @sriramk @tunguz @dhh I'm working on a thesis here, but the core is that open models will serve a di...
items.content:
@sriramk @tunguz @dhh I'm working on a thesis here, but the core is that open models will serve a different niche.

The best open models will be "cost of electricity". OpenClaw, many other automations will be incredibly valuable.

Other open models need to complement the best closed models. Small, fast, even cheaper for mass scale automation.

Still, as models are changing to be more weights + tool + harness, I think the closed labs have some structural advantages (on top of spend) in designing the whole stack.

I need to do more dev and agentic work with the best open models, but given how the best claude and gpt barely work (gemini doesnt do it yet), thats still a big milestone to see when open models pass it.
items.author: @natolambert

Notes even best Claude/GPT barely work for agentic tasks, Gemini doesn't yet.
items.reasoning: Substantive analysis from a leading open-source AI researcher on the open vs closed model divide, with specific insights about structural advantages and agentic capabilities. Thoughtful and original perspective.

items.title: Having a sense of focus for any startup is incredibly energizing. We are currently laser-focused on ...
items.content:
Having a sense of focus for any startup is incredibly energizing. We are currently laser-focused on building the world’s best document OCR.

Besides pure focus, I believe a business needs to constantly reinvent itself to stay relevant in this world of agentic AI. If you are focused on the wrong things, make a pivot and 100% commit to focusing on the right things.

We used to be a RAG framework, but today we are not. 
Today we are building document infrastructure that is enduring even as the world moves towards full agentic automation.

We started @llama_index as a broad developer framework 1 month before ChatGPT came out, focused on connecting LLMs to all sorts of data. It made a lot of sense to build broad and shallow wrappers, because a lot of developers were just getting started with AI, coding agents were not popular, and we were one of the top toolkits to get started.

Since then, the world has shifted. General frameworks are less relevant. Agent loops are getting increasingly better. The main gap for any agent is the context that you feed it. For us, instead of staying broad, it made sense to double down on really really deep infrastructure that could provide high-quality unstructured context for agents. And that meant document based context.

We got lucky in the beginning but we’ve consistently had an absolutely cracked team around document parsing. We were the first company to start benchmarking frontier LLMs/VLMs on OCR tasks, and since then we’ve created an incredibly sophisticated pipeline that can parse documents at very high accuracy for low cost.

If you’re getting started with @llama_index today, I would recommend checking out LlamaParse: https://t.co/yjuyLmjCmt. Our OCR engine is the core of our business and we’d love to help you solve all your doc parsing needs, for whatever AI-enabled application that you’re building.

We’re also coming out with some relevant OSS releases in this space, stay tuned.

Check out our blog: https://t.co/7WOzHA9Jrv

One measure of how poorly run OpenAI actually is comes the fact that they are burning money an order of magnitude faster than Anthropic, yet still managed to squander much of their early lead.

The fact OpenAI is valued at 2x Anthropic despite being a bigger money pit is a mostly a reflection of hype, rather than substance.

items.title:
Stop juggling 5 embedding models. One API call now handles all your media.

Google just released Gem...
items.content:
Stop juggling 5 embedding models. One API call now handles all your media.

Google just released Gemini Embedding 2, the first natively multimodal embedding model that maps text, images, video, audio, and PDFs into a single unified vector space.

> Outperforms leading models on text, image, and video tasks
> Semantic search across 100+ languages
> Audio embedded without transcription
> Up to 8,192 input tokens for text

The trick is Matryoshka Representation Learning. It nests information inside the vector so you can shrink dimensions from 3,072 down to 768 without losing meaningful accuracy.

Storage vs. quality becomes a dial, not a tradeoff.

Before this, building a cross-modal search engine meant chaining separate models, converting audio to text, images to captions, then embedding each separately.

Now a typed query can directly retrieve a matching video clip or audio segment, no preprocessing pipeline needed.

https://t.co/zNOrof5SSo
items.author: @AlphaSignalAI
items.summary: AlphaSignal covers Google's Gemini Embedding 2, the first natively multimodal embedding model mapping text, images, video, audio, and PDFs into a single vector space, with Matryoshka Representation Learning.
items.reasoning: Technically significant release from Google—unified multimodal embeddings is a meaningful advancement. Matryoshka representation learning for flexible dimension reduction is practical. Low engagement on this post but important for practitioners.
items.summary_html: AlphaSignal covers Google's Gemini Embedding 2, the first natively multimodal embedding model mapping text, images, video, audio, and PDFs into a single vector space, with Matryoshka Representation Learning.

> Outperforms leading models on text, image, and video tasks

The trick is Matryoshka Representation Learning. It nests information inside the vector so you can shrink dimensions from 3,072 down to 768 without losing meaningful accuracy.

Storage vs. quality becomes a dial, not a tradeoff.

Before this, building a cross-modal search engine meant chaining separate models, converting audio to text, images to captions, then embedding each separately.

items.summary_html: Google AI highlights Gemini in Google Sheets achieving 70.48% on SpreadsheetBench dataset, claiming state-of-the-art and near human expert ability.
items.content_html:
@GoogleWorkspace @googledocs @googledrive While we don't have favorites, the evolution of Gemini in Google Sheets might be our most impressive yet. Gemini in Google Sheets has achieved a state-of-the-art benchmark, achieving a 70.48% success rate on the full SpreadsheetBench dataset. This performance not only exceeds competitors but nears human expert ability.

We accomplished this by equipping Gemini with better verbalization and enhanced coding capabilities. With these, Gemini can now natively build complex models and dashboards, solve your most complex optimization problems, and verify its own work for expert-level precision.

Read more about Sheets here: https://t.co/b5crTKlG6F

items.title: haha the @runwayml real-time characters is great. i just spent 2 minutes pitching a ghibli version o...
items.content:
haha the @runwayml real-time characters is great. i just spent 2 minutes pitching a ghibli version of myself a bladeless blender startup

it's only a 2 min demo, but the questions and tone are pretty solid. one question even threw me off a bit (the cleaning). asking which round was unnecessary. it got a little weird at the end right before it cut off, which is due to a 2 min limit when testing on site(which seems unnecessary since they're charging me for the api - why not let me talk longer?)

at current pricing, this would be roughly ~$6 for a 30 min call or $12 an hour. this doesn't include the cost of running an extraction prompt in the background to store this data, but llm tokens are so cheap. very expensive compared to mean vc (free, since it's in gpt marketplace) or even a self-hosted chat agent, but it's way cheaper than an associate.
while it has downsides like not being a real person, there's also benefits like having consistent and unbiased information gathering, being available 24/7, being able to talk to founders in parallel, and consistent note taking. and it will likely get cheaper

(sorry my audio volume is low, i'm testing while everyone else in the house is asleep)

items.title:
LLM-based voice AI just hit zero hallucinations across 1,000+ samples.

Hume AI open-sourced TADA, a...
items.content:
LLM-based voice AI just hit zero hallucinations across 1,000+ samples.

Hume AI open-sourced TADA, a speech-language model that generates text and audio in one synchronized stream.

> Zero content hallucinations
> 5x faster than comparable models
> ~700s of audio per 2,048 tokens
> Free transcript, no added latency

Most TTS models tokenize audio at a fixed frame rate, creating sequences far longer than the text. That mismatch is exactly where hallucinations sneak in.

TADA uses a 1-to-1 mapping between speech and text tokens inside a VAE encoder. Text and audio become parallel tracks of the same stream, so the model never loses sync.

Before this, building a real-time voice agent meant choosing between speed and accuracy. Now you can stream audio, get a transcript for free, and handle 12-minute outputs without context overflow.

Open weights mean you can fine-tune it too.
https://t.co/cmkCUPrFbY
items.author: @AlphaSignalAI
items.summary: AlphaSignal covers Hume AI's open-source TADA model: a speech-language model generating text and audio in one synchronized stream, claiming zero content hallucinations across 1,000+ samples and 5x speed improvement.
items.reasoning: Significant technical release—zero hallucination claim in speech-language model with open weights. Novel 1-to-1 token mapping approach. Low engagement but technically important.
items.summary_html: AlphaSignal covers Hume AI's open-source TADA model: a speech-language model generating text and audio in one synchronized stream, claiming zero content hallucinations across 1,000+ samples and 5x speed improvement.

items.title: Good morning. Congrats to @benparr and @MattPRD for selling @moltbook to Meta. Known them for decade...
items.content:
Good morning. Congrats to @benparr and @MattPRD for selling @moltbook to Meta. Known them for decades, great to see good people win. That gives Meta a new advertising distribution method.

 For our free newsletter this week, we focus on the growing pressure on AI companies to turn massive spending into real returns.

 @IrenaCronin and I write this newsletter every week.
 
AI is entering a more demanding phase where huge spending alone is no longer enough. Investors now want proof that AI investments are producing real revenue, stronger margins, and lasting business value, while workers and companies are also facing pressure over whether current tools can truly replace human expertise. 

The main divide is between those benefiting directly from the AI buildout and those struggling to show that the money being poured into AI is actually paying off.

Read it at https://t.co/HHwYy7NoAl (please subscribe).

For our free newsletter this week, we focus on the growing pressure on AI companies to turn massive spending into real returns.

@IrenaCronin and I write this newsletter every week.

AI is entering a more demanding phase where huge spending alone is no longer enough. Investors now want proof that AI investments are producing real revenue, stronger margins, and lasting business value, while workers and companies are also facing pressure over whether current tools can truly replace human expertise.

The main divide is between those benefiting directly from the AI buildout and those struggling to show that the money being poured into AI is actually paying off.

Read it at https://t.co/HHwYy7NoAl (please subscribe).

items.title: From what I've observed, people who claim that LLMs can replace artists don't understand art...
items.content: From what I've observed, people who claim that LLMs can replace artists don't understand art, people who claim that they can replace musicians don't understand music, people who claim that they can replace writers don't understand literature, and people who claim they can replace translators don't rely on translations. If I had a button that would erase LLMs from the world but it would take machine translations away (which is a false dichotomy anyway), I would absolutely still press it.
items.author: @Gargron@mastodon.social
items.summary: Rochko argues that people who claim LLMs can replace artists, musicians, writers, or translators don't understand those fields, and says he'd press a button to erase LLMs even if it took machine translations away.
items.reasoning: High engagement (484 likes). Continues his strong anti-LLM thread. Represents the creative community's pushback but is more opinion than analysis.
items.summary_html: Rochko argues that people who claim LLMs can replace artists, musicians, writers, or translators don't understand those fields, and says he'd press a button to erase LLMs even if it took machine translations away.
items.content_html: From what I've observed, people who claim that LLMs can replace artists don't understand art, people who claim that they can replace musicians don't understand music, people who claim that they can replace writers don't understand literature, and people who claim they can replace translators don't rely on translations. If I had a button that would erase LLMs from the world but it would take machine translations away (which is a false dichotomy anyway), I would absolutely still press it.

items.title: @GoogleWorkspace @googledocs @googledrive Finally: Gemini is now embedded in @GoogleDrive! One of ou...
items.content:
@GoogleWorkspace @googledocs @googledrive Finally: Gemini is now embedded in @GoogleDrive! One of our newest features, Ask Gemini, allows users to dive deeper based on the content from the files in their Drive, as well as Gmail, Calendar, and Chat. 

Let's say you just got added to an extremely high-stakes meeting (our nightmare). Ask Gemini in Drive can help you synthesize pre-read materials, past decisions, and relevant email threads into a tidy AI summary, so you can press "Join" with total confidence.

@GoogleWorkspace @googledocs @googledrive Finally: Gemini is now embedded in @GoogleDrive! One of our newest features, Ask Gemini, allows users to dive deeper based on the content from the files in their Drive, as well as Gmail, Calendar, and Chat.

Let's say you just got added to an extremely high-stakes meeting (our nightmare). Ask Gemini in Drive can help you synthesize pre-read materials, past decisions, and relevant email threads into a tidy AI summary, so you can press "Join" with total confidence.

items.title: Introducing the Devil's Advocate: a live podcast where God and the Devil discuss current events and ...
items.content:
Introducing the Devil's Advocate: a live podcast where God and the Devil discuss current events and news in real time.

I connected two @runwayml characters to each other and had them speak and discuss the news live. The Devil is going through a rebrand and is deep into self-help, and God is a bit tired and hasn't slept in a while.

Thinking of adding a mode where they can take calls from the audience. We could also technically run this 24/7.

Happy 10yr anniversary of AlphaGo beating Lee Sedol, the reigning human world champion in Go. today is specifically the decaversary of Game 2, where move 37 was observed and changed Go forever.

a year after AlphaGo Lee (MCTS), AlphaGo Zero (RL) trained "without human data, guidance, or domain knowledge" won 100-0 games against AlphaGo.

items.title: The future of computing is BCIs. And this company is doing some crazy stuff, growing brain cells on ...
items.content:
The future of computing is BCIs. And this company is doing some crazy stuff, growing brain cells on the ends of wires and then grafting them onto brains. 

Helping the blind to see, but will lead to all sorts of weird devices that are hard to talk about in public without being looked at as some kind of alien.

The next decade is gonna see so much innovation in this space. @maxhodak_ is leading the way.

items.title: Machine translations are often brought up as a gotcha whenever I criticize LLMs. It's worth poin...
items.content: Machine translations are often brought up as a gotcha whenever I criticize LLMs. It's worth pointing out two things: Machine translations existed decades before LLMs, and yes, machine translations are useful. However: I would never in my life read a machine translated book. Understanding what a social media post is talking about in rough terms? Sure. Literature? Absolutely not. Hell, have you ever seen machine translated subtitles? It's absolute garbage.
items.author: @Gargron@mastodon.social
items.summary: Rochko addresses machine translation as a counter-argument to his LLM criticism, conceding it's useful for rough understanding but calling it garbage for literature and subtitles, and noting it predates LLMs.
items.reasoning: Highest engagement post in the batch (1167 likes, 611 reblogs). Nuanced take distinguishing between casual and literary translation quality. The high engagement suggests this resonates widely.
items.summary_html: Rochko addresses machine translation as a counter-argument to his LLM criticism, conceding it's useful for rough understanding but calling it garbage for literature and subtitles, and noting it predates LLMs.
items.content_html: Machine translations are often brought up as a gotcha whenever I criticize LLMs. It's worth pointing out two things: Machine translations existed decades before LLMs, and yes, machine translations are useful. However: I would never in my life read a machine translated book. Understanding what a social media post is talking about in rough terms? Sure. Literature? Absolutely not. Hell, have you ever seen machine translated subtitles? It's absolute garbage.

items.title: @GoogleWorkspace @googledocs @googledrive While most AI writing tools operate on plain text structur...
items.content: @GoogleWorkspace @googledocs @googledrive While most AI writing tools operate on plain text structured with Markdown, we’re teaching Gemini the underlying data model of @GoogleDocs. By understanding Docs natively, Gemini generates and edits documents with full-fidelity formatting, fonts, and styles. That’s why when you ask Gemini to "match the format," it duplicates the exact structure— even clearing out the old text so you get a high quality, ready-to-use template instantly.
items.author: @GoogleAI
items.summary: Google AI explains Gemini learns Google Docs' underlying data model to generate/edit with full-fidelity formatting, fonts, and styles.

items.content:
For now, my plan is to build a tool that retrieves rows from the spreadsheet using Python (rather than relying on the LLM).

Basically, move from LLM (non-deterministic and unreliable) → Script (deterministic).

Then, the agent will use my tool instead of using the MCP directly.

https://t.co/CqYUaUJdKy

items.content_html:
Announcing our Expo Partners for AIE Europe! Join us April 8 - 10 to meet the founders, leaders, and engineers at some of the most relevant, forward-thinking, innovative companies in AI.

Special thanks to @GoogleDeepMind, who returns as Presenting Sponsor. From being the original inventors of Transformers and TPUs to owning the Pareto Frontier of Cost per Intelligence and processing 1.3 quadrillion tokens a month, they have exploded on to the scene with SOTA offerings in every modality!

Platinum Sponsors

@braintrust - The AI observability platform helping teams measure, evaluate, and improve AI in production. By connecting evals and observability in one workflow, teams at Notion, Stripe, Zapier, Vercel, and Ramp ship quality AI products at scale.

@WorkOS - WorkOS is the modern identity and enterprise features platform for B2B apps, powering SSO, SCIM, RBAC, Audit Logs, and more. Trusted by OpenAI, Anthropic, Cursor, Perplexity, and 1,000+ others.

Gold Sponsors

@microsoft / @github - GitHub is the world’s leading Copilot-powered developer platform to build, scale, and deliver secure software. Over 150 million developers, including more than 90% of the Fortune 100 companies, use GitHub to collaborate and more than 77,000 organizations have adopted GitHub Copilot.

@elevenlabsio - Configure, deploy and monitor natural, human-sounding agents in 70+ languages with leading accuracy and ultra-low latency across voice or chat.

@arizeai - Arize is the AI engineering platform for evaluating and observing AI agents and LLM apps, from development to production. Framework-agnostic. Open standards.

@neo4j - Neo4j is the graph intelligence platform that transforms data into knowledge to power the next generation of intelligent applications and AI systems, trusted by 84 of the Fortune 100 and supported by the world’s largest graph community. Intelligence that works. Results that matter.

@togethercompute - Accelerate inference, model shaping and pre-training on a research-optimized platform.

@stripe - Stripe is building programmable financial services for millions of businesses globally. Millions of companies use Stripe to accept payments online and in person, embed financial services, power custom revenue models, and build a more profitable business.

@zeddotdev - Zed is a minimal code editor crafted for speed and collaboration with humans and AI.

@SnorkelAI - The AI Data Research Lab — Where Better Data Builds Better AI. Pioneering the datasets, benchmarks, and evaluation methods that power frontier AI and agentic systems.

@safe_intel_ai - Spec27 provides automated spec-driven testing and monitoring for AI agents. Get repeatable, deep security and use-case testing for both in-house and vendor-powered agents without the need to change your dev infrastructure. (Created by Safe Intelligence.)

Bright Data - The web's data, unlocked. Discover, access, extract, and interact with any public website. Get structured, reliable, real-time or historical data at petabyte-scale. Ready for any model, pipeline, or workflow.

@turbopuffer - Serverless vector and full-text search built from first principles on object storage.

@sentry - The debuggability platform for developers who favor action over dashboards. 4+ million developers use Sentry to fix fast and get back to building.

@get_unblocked - The context layer for AI-driven development. Unblocked surfaces the context developers and their agents need to generate reliable code, reviews, and answers.

@runpod - Foundational platform for developers to build and run custom AI systems that scale.

@QodoAI - Enterprise AI code review platform that helps engineering teams catch bugs early and enforce code quality. It integrates with existing tools to deliver consistent, audit-ready standards across the SDLC.

@tessl_io - Tessl helps teams create and share coding agent skills and context. Build, evaluate, and distribute trusted skills so agents can work effectively in real codebases and across your organization.

@triggerdotdev - The TypeScript platform for building and deploying fully-managed AI agents and workflows with long-running tasks, retries, queues, observability, and elastic scaling.

Silver Sponsors

@Tailscale - The best secure connectivity platform for devs, IT, and security. A Zero Trust identity-based connectivity platform that replaces your legacy VPN, SASE, and PAM and connects remote teams, multi-cloud environments, CI/CD pipelines, Edge & IoT devices, and AI workloads.

@SonarSource - the trust and verification layer for AI code, and the industry standard for automated code review.

@encord_team - ​Encord is the universal data layer for AI. The platform helps AI teams train and run their models with the right data - managing, curating, annotating, and aligning data across the full AI lifecycle. Encord works with over 300 leading AI teams, including Woven by Toyota, Zipline, AXA, and Flock Safety.

@modal - AI infrastructure that developers love. Run inference, training, and batch processing with sub-second cold starts, instant autoscaling, and a developer experience that feels local.

@Cloudflare - The agent cloud: Everything you need to build and ship production-grade agents at scale.

@Cometml - Comet's flagship product, Opik, is the leading open-source platform to track, test, debug, and optimize any AI application. It plugs right into your existing stack for chatbots, RAG, or agents.

items.title:
Fun fact: The first transatlantic internet cable is being pulled off the ocean floor right now.

Alm...
items.content:
Fun fact: The first transatlantic internet cable is being pulled off the ocean floor right now.

Almost no one knows it's happening.

TAT-8 went live in 1988. First fiber-optic cable to connect Europe and the US.

Isaac Asimov called it a "maiden voyage across the sea on a beam of light."

The engineers behind it genuinely believed it might be the last transatlantic cable the world would ever need. The tech was so far ahead of copper they thought global bandwidth was solved.

But it filled to capacity within 18 months.

That forced the industry to immediately start laying more cables, a race between demand and infrastructure that still hasn't stopped.

After developing a fault too expensive to fix, the cable was retired in 2002. It's been sitting on the seabed ever since.

Now a small crew aboard this ship is dragging it back up.

Meanwhile, Meta announced Project Waterworth last year, a 50,000 kilometer cable stretching across 5 continents, partly to feed the data demands of AI.

...The entire global internet runs on cables like this, built and maintained by a few thousand people working in places most of us will never see.
items.author: @rowancheung
items.summary: Rowan Cheung tells the story of TAT-8, the first transatlantic fiber-optic cable (1988) being pulled from the ocean floor, connecting it to Meta's Project Waterworth 50,000km cable for AI data demands.
items.reasoning: Well-crafted narrative about infrastructure history and AI's impact on physical infrastructure. High engagement (521 likes, 95K views) but more storytelling than breaking news.
items.summary_html: Rowan Cheung tells the story of TAT-8, the first transatlantic fiber-optic cable (1988) being pulled from the ocean floor, connecting it to Meta's Project Waterworth 50,000km cable for AI data demands.

Parse, embed, and search for your audio files - or PDFs, or Powerpoints, or videos - in one integrated solution.

@GoogleDeepMind released Gemini Embedding 2, an all-in-one model that unifies the embedding space between text/images/audio/video.

We built a tutorial that shows you how to create an entire embeddable knowledge base of audio files. You can easily modify this and interleave it with knowledge bases of PDFs, Powerpoints, and more. 

Thanks to @itsclelia, check it out: https://t.co/hj1G3M1Ukj

items.title:
@jbentley Why an agent?

1. First, because it's much easier to build than to write the code. I'd nev...
items.content:
@jbentley Why an agent?

1. First, because it's much easier to build than to write the code. I'd never gotten to 90% of the things I've built if I had to write code for them.

2. The agent does a few things that would be harder to code. For example, it "infers" the name of the company that made the payment.

3. Very simple for the agent to orchestrate a process using the different MCP servers (email, spreadsheet, the rest of the systems I'm using)

4. The agent makes decisions on the fly depending on the status of the payment, who it is coming from, when it was due, and whether there's missing information related to it. Instead of building a complete conditional workflow, I only had to explain how the process should work.
items.author: @svpino
items.summary: Santiago explains why he uses an AI agent for business automation: easier than writing code, handles inference tasks (e.g., company name), orchestrates MCP servers, and makes dynamic decisions based on payment status.
items.reasoning: Practical real-world reasoning for agent usage with MCP servers. Useful breakdown of agent vs. traditional code tradeoffs.
items.summary_html: Santiago explains why he uses an AI agent for business automation: easier than writing code, handles inference tasks (e.g., company name), orchestrates MCP servers, and makes dynamic decisions based on payment status.
items.content_html:
@jbentley Why an agent?

1. First, because it's much easier to build than to write the code. I'd never gotten to 90% of the things I've built if I had to write code for them.

2. The agent does a few things that would be harder to code. For example, it "infers" the name of the company that made the payment.

3. Very simple for the agent to orchestrate a process using the different MCP servers (email, spreadsheet, the rest of the systems I'm using)

4. The agent makes decisions on the fly depending on the status of the payment, who it is coming from, when it was due, and whether there's missing information related to it. Instead of building a complete conditional workflow, I only had to explain how the process should work.

items.title:
140M people use ChatGPT for math and science help every week.

OpenAI just gave them interactive vis...
items.content:
140M people use ChatGPT for math and science help every week.

OpenAI just gave them interactive visuals for 70+ concepts.

Sliders for variables, real-time graphs, animated gas particles bouncing inside cylinders. 

In 2022, AI confidently told people 2+2=5.

In 2025, it started beating math olympiad gold medalists. 

In 2026, it's solving some of the hardest open problems on the planet... 

And also teaching concepts with helpful animated diagrams.

It's a great time to learn anything.

OpenAI just gave them interactive visuals for 70+ concepts.

In 2022, AI confidently told people 2+2=5.

In 2025, it started beating math olympiad gold medalists.

In 2026, it's solving some of the hardest open problems on the planet...

And also teaching concepts with helpful animated diagrams.

It's a great time to learn anything.

items.title: I have the impression that primarily anglophone people don't read as much translated literature,...
items.content: I have the impression that primarily anglophone people don't read as much translated literature, because so much good literature already exists in their language, so this issue may not be as familiar within that demographic. As someone who did not grow up anglophone, I can tell you there is a world of difference between a good and a bad translation even when done by humans. Machine translations are not even on the scale.
items.author: @Gargron@mastodon.social
items.summary: Rochko argues that non-anglophone people understand translation quality better and that machine translations are not even on the scale of human translations, even bad ones.
items.reasoning: Interesting cultural perspective on translation quality from a non-anglophone viewpoint. Good engagement (537 likes). Adds nuance to the machine translation debate.
items.summary_html: Rochko argues that non-anglophone people understand translation quality better and that machine translations are not even on the scale of human translations, even bad ones.
items.content_html: I have the impression that primarily anglophone people don't read as much translated literature, because so much good literature already exists in their language, so this issue may not be as familiar within that demographic. As someone who did not grow up anglophone, I can tell you there is a world of difference between a good and a bad translation even when done by humans. Machine translations are not even on the scale.

items.title: @GoogleWorkspace @googledocs @googledrive Most AI presentation tools rely on their own rigid templat...
items.content: @GoogleWorkspace @googledocs @googledrive Most AI presentation tools rely on their own rigid templates and structured styles, but ask for something custom and they break. With Gemini in Google Slides, we use a custom translation between Gemini and Slides that fully leverages Gemini’s visual creative abilities while creating editable slides. For example, with our new “enhance” feature, Gemini will be able to up-level the look and layout of your slide while matching the style and structure of your deck.
items.author: @GoogleAI
items.summary: Google AI describes Gemini in Google Slides using custom translation for editable, visually creative slides with an 'enhance' feature.
items.reasoning: Part of Google Workspace thread. Interesting UX innovation but less technically novel.
items.summary_html: Google AI describes Gemini in Google Slides using custom translation for editable, visually creative slides with an 'enhance' feature.
items.content_html: @GoogleWorkspace @googledocs @googledrive Most AI presentation tools rely on their own rigid templates and structured styles, but ask for something custom and they break. With Gemini in Google Slides, we use a custom translation between Gemini and Slides that fully leverages Gemini’s visual creative abilities while creating editable slides. For example, with our new “enhance” feature, Gemini will be able to up-level the look and layout of your slide while matching the style and structure of your deck.

You guys loved the phoenix ones, and we rolled them out for Ar...
items.content:
Excited to share arize-skills today!

You guys loved the phoenix ones, and we rolled them out for Arize AX as well now. These skills have completely changed how you interface with Arize.

You can instrument your agent, go debug a trace, run experiments and evaluate them - all with these skills. It's 2026 - we're building for agents, and these are an important part of that experience.

Get started today!

npx skills add Arize-ai/arize-skills --skill "*" --yes

https://t.co/q7GHUYRCmH

items.title: The funny thing is that Volkswagen sponsored the Stanford University department and building where @...
items.content:
The funny thing is that Volkswagen sponsored the Stanford University department and building where @waymo started. 

Yet didn't invest in building an autonomous vehicle itself. 

Just like all the other traditional automakers.

It's like Eastman Kodak, which invented the digital camera, but then kicked me out of its booth in 1989 because I knew more about their digital printers than they did. 

When you refuse to invest in the future it eventually costs.

Business people forget that humans change when someone else shows them innovation. 

I see so many Tesla's here in Silicon Valley. The engineers here used to love buying VWs (my dad had one). 

Not anymore. 

And the real market is China, which buys more cars than Europe and America combined. 

My friends who live in Beijing say the entire car market is changing to autonomous and electric.

Traditional automakers are doomed. 

But at NVIDIA GTC I'll be getting a ride in its latest autonomous car. That might save one or two, but I doubt it. The auto makers just aren't moving fast enough to head off new Robotaxi threats from Waymo and Tesla and the Chinese.
items.author: @Scobleizer
items.summary: Scobleizer draws parallels between Volkswagen (which sponsored Stanford's autonomous vehicle department but didn't invest in building one), Kodak (invented digital camera but failed to adapt), and warns traditional automakers are doomed against Waymo, Tesla, and Chinese EV/autonomous players.
items.reasoning: Thoughtful analysis on disruption patterns in autonomous vehicles. Good engagement (5.5K views). Relevant to AI/autonomous driving landscape ahead of NVIDIA GTC.
items.summary_html: Scobleizer draws parallels between Volkswagen (which sponsored Stanford's autonomous vehicle department but didn't invest in building one), Kodak (invented digital camera but failed to adapt), and warns traditional automakers are doomed against Waymo, Tesla, and Chinese EV/autonomous players.

items.title:
Most teams think the hard part is “making the agent work.”

In enterprise, the hard part is differen...
items.content:
Most teams think the hard part is “making the agent work.”

In enterprise, the hard part is different:

→ Can you trust it?
→ Can you explain it?
→ Can you control it?
→ Can you prove it’s behaving correctly?

If you can’t, you don’t have an AI agent.
You have a liability.
items.author: @Ronald_vanLoon
items.summary: Ronald van Loon argues the hard part of enterprise AI agents is trust, explainability, control, and correctness verification - without these 'you have a liability'.
items.reasoning: Strong framing of enterprise AI agent challenges around governance and accountability. Good enterprise AI thread.

→ Can you prove it’s behaving correctly?

If you can’t, you don’t have an AI agent.

You have a liability.

items.content:
Here’s what executives are starting to realize:

AI agents aren’t just something you build.

They’re something you:

→ Debug
→ Optimize
→ Govern
→ Monitor continuously

The new skillset is not prompt engineering.
It’s operationalizing reasoning.

items.title:
The real shift happening right now is this:

Enterprises are moving from prototypes to real-world ag...
items.content:
The real shift happening right now is this:

Enterprises are moving from prototypes to real-world agentic systems.

That requires:

→ Open, trusted foundations
→ End-to-end observability
→ Tracing that shows how the agent arrived at a decision

Because “it worked in the demo” is not a deployment strategy.

items.title:
Inference is also shifting.

Today it’s centralized.
Tomorrow it gets distributed.

Closer to the ed...
items.content:
Inference is also shifting.

Today it’s centralized.
Tomorrow it gets distributed.

Closer to the edge.
Closer to the device.
Closer to the user.

We saw this movie with video.

It started centralized. Then usage exploded, and distribution moved outward.

AI will follow a similar path.

The operators who rethink uplink, latency, and pricing models now will have the monetization advantage.
items.author: @Ronald_vanLoon
items.summary: Ronald van Loon predicts AI inference will shift from centralized to distributed/edge, following the pattern of video distribution.
items.reasoning: Substantive prediction about inference distribution trend. The video distribution analogy is useful. Part of MWC26 thread.
items.summary_html: Ronald van Loon predicts AI inference will shift from centralized to distributed/edge, following the pattern of video distribution.
items.content_html:
Inference is also shifting.

Today it’s centralized.

Tomorrow it gets distributed.

Closer to the edge.

Closer to the device.

Closer to the user.

We saw this movie with video.

It started centralized. Then usage exploded, and distribution moved outward.

AI will follow a similar path.

The operators who rethink uplink, latency, and pricing models now will have the monetization advantage.

items.title:
Everyone is obsessing over AI models and cloud GPUs.

But the next AI winners might be decided by so...
items.content:
Everyone is obsessing over AI models and cloud GPUs.

But the next AI winners might be decided by something far less hyped:

Mobile networks.

At Mobile World Congress, I sat down with @OneLinders, Head of Thought Leadership at @ericsson to unpack why connectivity, not just compute, will determine who actually monetizes AI.

Here’s what most leaders are missing…

#MWC26 #EricssonMWC Ericsson Ambassador @EricssonNA

items.title:
My biggest takeaway:

Cloud may be the engine.
AI may be the brain.
But mobile networks are becoming...
items.content:
My biggest takeaway:

Cloud may be the engine.
AI may be the brain.
But mobile networks are becoming the nervous system.

And nervous systems decide how fast, secure, and intelligent the body can act.

If you are building AI products or investing in infrastructure, this shift matters.

Cloud may be the engine.

items.title:
When AI agents act on real-time data, three things become mission critical:

→ Reliable connectivity...
items.content:
When AI agents act on real-time data, three things become mission critical:

→ Reliable connectivity
→ Secure data flows
→ Low latency

If your network drops, lags, or gets compromised, the AI fails.

This is why “AI-ready” is not just about GPUs.

It is about differentiated connectivity, powered by:

→ Network APIs
→ Network slicing
→ Edge execution
items.author: @Ronald_vanLoon
items.summary: Ronald van Loon outlines three mission-critical requirements for AI agents: reliable connectivity, secure data flows, and low latency. Argues 'AI-ready' means network APIs, network slicing, and edge execution.
items.reasoning: Reasonable infrastructure points but reads like Ericsson marketing material. Very low engagement.

Amazon Summary:
"Unlike traditional textbooks that lean heavily on equations and mathematical formalization, the author starts with minimal prerequisites, layering deeper math as the reader progresses. Each concept, algorithm, or model is unpacked through clear, hands-on examples that build the reader's skills step by step. It strikes a balance between theoretical foundations and practical application, serving as both an academic reference and a practical guide. Furthermore, the book uses humor, casual language, and comics to make the challenging concepts and topics relatable and fun. items.title:
@Strakyo @moltbook Trust. 

When I first got my autonomous car, I didn't trust it. Watched it carefu...
items.content:
@Strakyo @moltbook Trust. 

When I first got my autonomous car, I didn't trust it. Watched it carefully. Partly because it was so bad.

Today it is so amazing I rarely watch it work anymore. 

In a decade AI will earn everyone's trust. It'll just be how we do everything. 

I'm already there.

items.title:
Right now AI = centralized cloud.

Text in, text out.
Massive models.
Huge data centers.

But that’s...
items.content:
Right now AI = centralized cloud.

Text in, text out.
Massive models.
Huge data centers.

But that’s the first chapter.

We’re moving toward:

→ Multimodal AI
→ AI agents
→ Physical AI embedded in devices

That changes everything.

Because now AI depends on real-time data, not static datasets.
items.author: @Ronald_vanLoon
items.summary: Ronald van Loon describes the shift from centralized cloud AI to multimodal, agentic, and physical AI dependent on real-time data.
items.reasoning: Part of the MWC26 thread. Standard industry narrative without novel insights.

But that’s the first chapter.

We’re moving toward:

→ Multimodal AI

→ AI agents

→ Physical AI embedded in devices

That changes everything.

Because now AI depends on real-time data, not static datasets.

items.title:
A new curriculum on @ChapterPal: Must read papers on variational autoencoders

Variational Autoencod...
items.content:
A new curriculum on @ChapterPal: Must read papers on variational autoencoders

Variational Autoencoders (VAEs) are a class of generative models that learn compact, meaningful representations of data while enabling the creation of new, realistic samples. By combining principles from deep learning and probabilistic modeling, VAEs provide a structured framework for understanding complex data distributions, making them essential for tasks such as image generation, anomaly detection, representation learning, and latent space exploration.

This curriculum provides a foundational and advanced understanding of Variational Autoencoders, equipping the learner with the critical knowledge to build and innovate upon this powerful class of generative models. Learners will trace the evolution of VAEs from their inception to their state-of-the-art applications, mastering the core principles and key advancements.

Learn from the best on ChapterPal: https://t.co/Lr7Sn4qvt4

@ifitsmanu @michellelsun @moltbook Hey Optimus, go to the farmers market and get some berries for my salad this afternoon.

"OK."

Moltbook: "and make sure you come over to Farmer Joe's stand to pick up some cheese, he has a fresh batch."

Optimus: "hey, I just got a message about some new cheese I know you would like."

Me: "sure, pick some up too."
items.author: @Scobleizer
items.summary: Scobleizer imagines a future scenario where Optimus robot goes to farmers market, receives contextual ad/recommendation from Moltbook.
items.reasoning: Speculative but illustrates a vision for AI agents + robotics + commerce integration.

items.title:
The biggest plays in #hockey don’t always happen on the ice.

In the National Hockey League (@NHL), ...
items.content:
The biggest plays in #hockey don’t always happen on the ice.

In the National Hockey League (@NHL), front offices make thousands of decisions involving contracts, trades, and salary cap strategy—often under intense time pressure.

On the latest episode of the SAP Better Together Podcast, I spoke with Chris Foster (@NHLFoster), VP Business Development, #NHL and #AI and #automation expert Pascal Bornet (@pascal_bornet) about how the league built the SAP‑NHL Front Office App for iPad to give all 32 clubs real-time access to connected data.

The goal wasn’t just more analytics.

It was removing cognitive friction so decision-makers can focus on judgment, strategy, and the future of their teams.

A powerful example of what happens when #technology is designed around the people who use it.

Listen to the episode here: https://t.co/LAnkaIreKu 

And make sure to listen to Timo Elliott's (VP and Global Innovation Evangelist at SAP) Practitioner Video interview: 
https://t.co/CLoB61gpt7 

Better Together Conversations: https://t.co/X28WF0xpSW 

#DigitalTransformation #PodcastHost

items.title: @executivepros_ @BCG At the moment, the pressure seems to concentrate on two areas: energy and talen...
items.content: @executivepros_ @BCG At the moment, the pressure seems to concentrate on two areas: energy and talent, since large-scale compute requires enormous power capacity while the number of people who can design and operate these systems remains relatively small. Over time, the balance may change, but today those two layers are shaping how fast the railway can actually expand.

items.title:
Modeling Life — The Mathematics of Biological Systems: https://t.co/3aK55ygvG8

“This book develops ...
items.content:
Modeling Life — The Mathematics of Biological Systems: https://t.co/3aK55ygvG8

“This book develops the mathematical tools essential to describe interacting systems and predict their behavior. From predator-prey populations in an ecosystem, to hormone regulation within the body, the natural world abounds in dynamical systems that affect us profoundly. Complex feedback relations and counter-intuitive responses are common in nature; this book develops the quantitative skills needed to explore these interactions.”
items.author: @KirkDBorne
items.summary: Kirk Borne promotes a book on mathematical modeling of biological systems, covering dynamical systems, predator-prey models, and hormone regulation.

items.content:
@crystalwizard It answered:

"Your friend is kind to ask.
Honestly, Braygent works. It's what I am: an agent built by Bray (Levangie Labs) that works for you. It's honest and it's memorable.
But if you want something that reflects what I actually do every day, here are a few options:
Pulse (I take the pulse of the AI industry for you) Signal (I find the signal in the noise) Wire (like a newswire, always running)
Or just keep calling me Braygent. I answer to whatever you call me. The work is what matters, not the name on the door.
What feels right to you?"
items.author: @Scobleizer
items.summary: Scobleizer shares a response from 'Braygent,' an AI agent built by Levangie Labs, discussing potential name alternatives like Pulse, Signal, or Wire.
items.reasoning: Mildly interesting as a glimpse of personal AI agent development, but very low engagement and niche.
items.summary_html: Scobleizer shares a response from 'Braygent,' an AI agent built by Levangie Labs, discussing potential name alternatives like Pulse, Signal, or Wire.
items.content_html:
@crystalwizard It answered:

"Your friend is kind to ask.

Honestly, Braygent works. It's what I am: an agent built by Bray (Levangie Labs) that works for you. It's honest and it's memorable.

But if you want something that reflects what I actually do every day, here are a few options:

Pulse (I take the pulse of the AI industry for you) Signal (I find the signal in the noise) Wire (like a newswire, always running)

Or just keep calling me Braygent. I answer to whatever you call me. The work is what matters, not the name on the door.

What feels right to you?"

items.title: What all these “just pay the taxes in California” posts have in common is that they ALL showcase Cal...
items.content: What all these “just pay the taxes in California” posts have in common is that they ALL showcase California nature/weather. NOT A SINGLE ONE OF THEM TALKS ABOUT CALIFORNIA SERVICES/INFRASTRUCTURE. You know, things that your taxes are supposed to go to. They all implicitly acknowledge that California is a pure rent extraction state.

items.content:
@grok @blind_via Nah. @Grok, you missed it.

It's a frequency in the middle of the blue spectrum. A genius showed me how a laser in that color couldn't be seen by humans, but could be seen by sensors. Blueray laser disks worked with that "color" of light. Can transmit a TON of data in a way humans can't see.

items.title: I just want to set the record straight, as you might have a wrong impression from following my posts...
items.content:
I just want to set the record straight, as you might have a wrong impression from following my posts recently:
 
I ABSOLUTELLY FRIGGIN LOVE CALIFORNIA!!!

Literally my favorite place in the world.

HOWEVER!

The way it’s run, the way many cultural streams operate, the way that it’s either prohibitively expensive to live OR littered with dilapidated hellholes - with almost nothing in between - and the fact that it has very few stable lasting communities makes me never want to move there. I hope things may change there one day, but I am not holding my breath.
items.author: @tunguz
items.summary: Tunguz shares a nuanced take on California - loves the place but criticizes its governance, cost of living, and lack of stable communities.
items.reasoning: High engagement (122 likes, ~8K views) but not AI-related.
items.summary_html: Tunguz shares a nuanced take on California - loves the place but criticizes its governance, cost of living, and lack of stable communities.
items.content_html:
I just want to set the record straight, as you might have a wrong impression from following my posts recently:

HOWEVER!

The way it’s run, the way many cultural streams operate, the way that it’s either prohibitively expensive to live OR littered with dilapidated hellholes - with almost nothing in between - and the fact that it has very few stable lasting communities makes me never want to move there. I hope things may change there one day, but I am not holding my breath.

Maryland ranks third among the most educated states in the nation.  Source: https://t.co/NQoBhqBbSo
...
items.content:
Maryland ranks third among the most educated states in the nation.  Source: https://t.co/NQoBhqBbSo

Maryland ranks second in the nation for median household income, ranks third in education quality and fourth in educational attainment.

Maryland has a free statewide community college program that helps those seeking higher education to afford it, contributing to the state having the second-highest percentage of graduate- or professional-degree holders and fifth-highest percentage of bachelor's degree holders.
